danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy plane sprites moving toward player from right side of screen
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion or destruction
Immediate damage risk; requires dodging or shield absorption
Accumulated damage; continued hits will result in pilot loss

danger_signals.visual_form: Large enemy unit appearing (military vehicle, fortification, or aircraft)
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat requiring sustained engagement; stage progression blocked until defeated

safety_signals.visual_form: Menu screen between levels displaying purchasable weapons and aircraft
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone; player can upgrade defenses and weapons without threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage end screen with money conversion UI
safety_signals.meaning: Safe transition; unused weapons convert to funds for next stage preparation

safety_signals.visual_form: Clear screen without enemy sprites or projectiles
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary safety; enemy spawning patterns allow brief respite

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: U.N. designation; military aircraft (F8 Crusader, etc.)
Enemy military hardware (hostile aircraft, ground vehicles, fortifications)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct enemy sprite designs differentiated from player aircraft
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military installations, airfields, ground-based defensive structures

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dense enemy spawning, projectile patterns on screen, explosions visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy aircraft attack in formation; ground units fire defensively; bosses engage with complex attack patterns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shop menu with available aircraft and weapons displayed; money counter updated based on stage performance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No enemy threats present; player given agency to select equipment before next engagement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Scrolling environment showing enemy positions, military installations, terrain variety
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters mark territorial strongholds; defeating bosses advances map progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Energy bar on HUD showing current damage absorption capacity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Pilot remains airborne until energy depleted; single energy bar per life (unlike traditional 1-hit systems)

environmental_storytelling.example: Ground-based defensive structures, airfields, and fortifications appear as stage backgrounds/obstacles, indicating Project 4 territorial presence

Three playable pilots (Shin Kazama, Mickey Simon, Greg Gates) each fly distinct aircraft types; SNES version allows all pilots to purchase varied planes, suggesting mercenary flexibility and resource-based progression
Money earned from destroying enemy units; unused weapons convert to funds; shop system implies supply-line and logistical gameplay, reinforcing mercenary-for-hire narrative
Boss encounters feature visually larger, more heavily-armed units than standard enemies, indicating command-level targets or strategic chokepoints

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to input; can move laterally, fire weapons, absorb damage via energy bar system
Spawns in patterns; moves toward player; fires projectiles; travels in formations
Stationary or semi-mobile; fires at player aircraft; appears in fortified positions
Large sprite with increased health; complex attack patterns; requires sustained fire; blocks stage progression until defeated
Can be attacked for points/items; environmental hazards that signal military installations

notes: U.N. Squadron (Area 88 in Japan) communicates threat and world-state primarily through sprite-based visual clarity: enemy types are immediately visually distinct, environmental military infrastructure indicates territorial control, and the shop system between levels reinforces a mercenary narrative where resource management reflects conflict progression. The energy bar system (shared damage pool per life) is a key mechanical-to-visual signal differentiating this from single-hit-death shmups. Pilot selection and aircraft variety suggest tactical choice. No explicit faction color-coding documented in available sources; distinction relies on sprite design and positioning.
